title: Where the Compound Interest Formula Comes From

md: Put $P$ dollars in an account that earns rate $r$ each year. After $n$ years the balance is:
Why a *power*? Powers usually come from repeated multiplication — so somewhere, something must be multiplying over and over. Let's find it.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: With principal $P$ and interest rate $r$ compounded each period, the balance after $n$ periods is $\;A = P(1+r)^n.$

md: In one period the account earns interest $r \times (\text{current balance})$. Starting from $P$, the new balance is the old balance **plus** the interest:
So one period doesn't *add* a fixed amount — it **multiplies the balance by $(1+r)$**. That single factor is the engine of the whole formula.
### Step 2 — Do it again, and again
Here's the crucial point: in period 2, the interest is charged on the *new, larger* balance — not the original $P$. So we multiply by $(1+r)$ **again**:

blocks.tex: \begin{aligned} \text{start:} \quad & P \\ \text{after 1 period:} \quad & P(1+r) \\ \text{after 2 periods:} \quad & P(1+r)(1+r) = P(1+r)^2 \\ \text{after 3 periods:} \quad & P(1+r)^3 \end{aligned}

md: Every period stamps on one more factor of $(1+r)$. After $n$ periods, $n$ of them have piled up:
### Compound vs. simple: why the gap explodes
**Simple** interest always charges $r$ on the *original* $P$ — a fixed $Pr$ added each period. That's repeated *adding*, so it grows in a straight line: $A = P(1 + rn)$.

**Compound** interest charges $r$ on the *current* balance — repeated *multiplying* — so it curves upward. The difference is "interest earning interest." Slide the controls and watch the two pull apart:

blocks.title: The takeaway
blocks.body: Adding a fixed amount gives a line; multiplying by a fixed factor gives a power. Compound interest is exponential growth — and that one word is why starting to save early matters so much.
